data.title: FinWise targets $8M-$10M monthly credit enhanced growth while guiding $4M-$5M quarterly net charge-offs for noncredit enhanced loans
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: FinWise Bancorp (FINW) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Earlier this month, Jim Noone assumed the role of CEO of FinWise Bancorp in addition to serving as President and CEO of FinWise Bank,” and “as Executive Chairman, I will remain actively involved in long-term strategy, Board governance and Investor Relations,” while “this transition does not change our strategic direction” (Executive chairman Kent Landvatter).

  • “I want to start by addressing our earnings shortfall this quarter. It was primarily driven by an increase in charge-offs in our SBA portfolio, concentrated in a narrow set of legacy credits,” and “we expect these charge-offs to remain elevated over the next few quarters as those credits continue to be actively managed” (Chief executive officer James Noone).

  • “This quarter, originations were strong at $1.7 billion, and core expenses held flat, enabling us to grow tangible book value per share to $14.34 at the end of Q1,” alongside “the platform is scaling, the pipeline is strengthening and the trajectory of this business has not changed” (CEO Noone).

  • “Credit enhanced balances at quarter end stood at $109 million,” and management said the slower sequential growth “was below our guided pace of $8 million to $10 million per month,” adding, “we continue to expect organic growth of $8 million to $10 million per month on average for the full year, with the growth now skewed toward the middle and back half of 2026” (CEO Noone).

  • “The growth in interchange income this quarter to $703,000 from $310,000 last quarter reflects the early contributions from our credit card portfolio,” and “earlier this month, we announced a new program with Vera” (CEO Noone).

  • “FinWise reported net income of $2.7 million for the first quarter and diluted earnings per share of $0.20,” and results were “adversely impacted by lower gain on sale income, a negative change in our BFG investment valuation and a large provision for credit losses with our traditional banking portfolio” (Executive VP & CFO Robert Wahlman).

Outlook

  • “Originations through the first 4 weeks of April are tracking at a quarterly run rate of approximately $1.4 billion,” and management said it is “reaffirming $1.4 billion in quarterly loan originations as a baseline,” with a full-year framework where “annualizing this baseline and applying a 5% growth rate provides a reasonable outlook for full year 2026 originations” (CFO Wahlman).

  • “We remain comfortable with organic growth in credit enhanced balances of $8 million to $10 million on average per month for 2026,” while noting “quarterly results may be lumpy with growth skewed toward the middle and back half of the year” (CFO Wahlman).

  • “We anticipate an approximate range of $4 million to $5 million in net charge-offs for noncredit enhanced loans is a good quarterly number to use in your models for the remainder of this year,” and “we think there is potentially as much as $10 million in watch list loans that could migrate to nonperforming loans in the second quarter” (CFO Wahlman).

  • “We suggest using 27% in your modeling” for the tax rate (CFO Wahlman).

Financial Results

  • “Quarterly net charge-offs were $9.4 million in Q1 compared to $6.7 million in the prior quarter,” including “$2.2 million from our core portfolio, primarily SBA 7(a) loans retained balances,” while “NPL balances increased by $6.1 million sequentially, bringing our total NPL balance to $49.8 million at the end of the quarter” (CEO Noone).

  • “Provision for credit losses was $10.6 million for the first quarter compared to $17.7 million for the prior quarter,” and management reiterated that on credit enhanced loans “it’s fully offset by the recognition of future recoveries recorded as credit enhancement income in noninterest income” (CEO Noone).

  • “Net interest income grew to $28.1 million from the prior quarter’s $24.6 million,” and “net interest margin increased to 12.9% compared to 11.42% in the prior quarter,” while “net of the adjustment for credit enhanced program expenses, net interest margin was 7.15% compared to 7.85% in the prior quarter” (CFO Wahlman).

  • “Noninterest income was $14.6 million compared to the prior quarter’s $22.3 million,” and “noninterest expense was $28.3 million compared to $23.7 million in the prior quarter,” with “excluding the offsetting accounting effects of the credit enhanced loans, the efficiency ratio was 65.0% for Q1 2026 and 60.6% for Q4 2025” (CFO Wahlman).

  • “Total assets were $899.4 million” vs. “$977.1 million in the prior quarter,” and “total end of the period deposits were $674.9 million compared to $754.6 million in the prior quarter,” while “we continue to operate with a very strong capital position, reflected in a bank leverage ratio of 16.8%” (CFO Wahlman).

Q&A

  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: “can you help us size the remaining pool of these legacy SBA credits?”; Chief executive officer Noone: “it’s about $50 million in performing outstanding balances at the end of Q1 that carry these attributes,” and “these attributes are what has led to 75% of the MCOs.”

  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: asked about NIM and the excess spread allocation change; Executive VP & CFO Wahlman: “The change in estimate grosses up or increases the interest income… And an exact offsetting amount is recorded as an expense,” and “nothing really changes from this change in estimate.”

  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: asked whether the accounting change was driven by accountants/regulators; CFO Wahlman: “This was done based upon our own review, but it has been reviewed and discussed with our external accounting firm.”

  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: asked about credit enhanced loan duration and partner behavior; CEO Noone: “on average, it’s probably like 15 months,” and on slower Q1 growth “we know this stuff is lumpy sometimes… The guidance is intact for the full year.”

  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: questioned whether year-end credit enhanced balances implied by the monthly guide are still achievable; CFO Wahlman: “That is still our expectation, Andrew.”

  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: pressed on efficiency expectations; CFO Wahlman: “it would require us to have about a $2 million increase in revenue to get that efficiency ratio down to 60%… we will get it back down to the mid-50s.”

  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: asked where cards/payments wins show up; CFO Wahlman: “on the payments, it’s going to be coming through that fee line,” and “on the payment side, that business is frequently accompanied by significant deposits.”

  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: challenged the originations baseline vs. student seasonality; CEO Noone: “it’s the student lending seasonality that we are not accounting for.”

  • E-mail question via IR: impact from fintechs pursuing bank charters; CEO Noone: “There is no immediate change to our business from the regulatory applications they have filed,” and “the lending pipeline at FinWise is stronger than I’ve seen it in my 8 years here.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on legacy SBA credit cleanup, accounting impacts to NIM/expenses, and feasibility of credit enhanced growth, including “Do you still feel like you can achieve that by the end of the year?” (Andrew Terrell, Stephens).

  • Management tone was slightly positive but more guarded on credit, emphasizing containment and continuity, including “we expect these charge-offs to remain elevated over the next few quarters,” alongside “the platform is scaling, the pipeline is strengthening and the trajectory of this business has not changed” (CEO Noone).

  • Versus last quarter’s framing that “credit trends remain stable” (then-Bank CEO Noone), the current call used more explicit language around an “earnings shortfall,” elevated charge-offs, and more detailed segmentation of legacy SBA exposures, while maintaining guidance language around originations and credit enhanced growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Leadership messaging shifted from Kent Landvatter as “CEO & Chairman” to “Executive Chairman,” with Jim Noone now company CEO; management said, “This transition does not change our strategic direction” (Executive chairman Landvatter).

  • Credit discussion intensified: Q4 referenced servicing/administration refinements and indicated elevated impacts “should be viewed as a onetime event” (CFO Wahlman, prior call), while Q1 described SBA charge-offs “concentrated in a narrow set of legacy credits” and stated they are expected to “remain elevated over the next few quarters” (CEO Noone).

  • Guidance posture was largely consistent on baseline originations and credit enhanced monthly growth, while the quarterly net charge-off modeling range for noncredit enhanced loans moved to “$4 million to $5 million” (CFO Wahlman) versus “approximately $3.5 million” previously (CFO Wahlman, prior call).

Risks and Concerns

  • “We expect these charge-offs to remain elevated over the next few quarters” in SBA legacy credits, which management tied to “still elevated interest rates continuing to impact certain origination years” and specific tightened policy areas (CEO Noone).

  • Management flagged asset quality migration risk: “potentially as much as $10 million in watch list loans that could migrate to nonperforming loans in the second quarter” (CFO Wahlman).

  • Credit enhanced portfolio seasoning was positioned as expected: “It is reasonable to expect charge-offs to rise as the portfolio matures and grows,” while also stating “FinWise is fully reimbursed for any losses” through partner cash reserves (CEO Noone).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 results as pressured by SBA legacy-credit charge-offs while reiterating its multi-engine growth plan across lending, credit enhanced programs, and emerging cards/payments contributions. Guidance language remained centered on a $1.4 billion quarterly originations baseline with a 5% annual growth framework and $8 million to $10 million average monthly credit enhanced balance growth, with growth timing “skewed toward the middle and back half of 2026,” alongside a higher modeled range for quarterly net charge-offs in noncredit enhanced loans and explicit expectations for elevated SBA charge-offs over the next few quarters.

data.title: Tetra Tech forecasts FY 2026 adjusted EPS of $1.50-$1.58 as backlog reaches $4.28B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tetra Tech (TTEK) Q2 FY 2026

Management View

  • “I’m pleased to join you today for my first quarterly call as CEO of Tetra Tech,” Roger Argus said, adding, “Demand for clean water, environmental quality and resilient infrastructure continues to grow worldwide. Our strategy is not changing.”

  • Argus reported “net revenue increased by 8%” and said “EBITDA of $146 million resulted in a margin expansion of 90 basis points ... and is an all-time record for a second quarter.” He added, “Our backlog increased by 8% sequentially and is now $4.28 billion,” and said adjusted EPS was “$0.34,” while EPS was “$0.36, including $0.02 associated with the completion of the divestiture of our Norwegian operations.”

  • Argus described segment performance: “The Government Services Group, or GSG, grew 5% ... and generated a margin of 16.3%,” while “the Commercial International Group or CIG ... performed well with revenue up 10% ... and a margin of 12.2%.”

  • Steven Burdick said year-to-date “adjusted EBITDA on net revenue for the quarter year-to-date increased by 110 basis points to 14% for the first half of fiscal 2026,” and highlighted cash execution: “cash flows generated from operations for the first half of the year were a historical record at $238 million.” Burdick also cited “DSO ... 58 days,” “net debt ... about $657 million,” leverage of “1.0x,” trailing 12-month operating cash flow of “$688 million,” and “return on capital employed ... over 20%.”

  • Burdick announced capital returns and deal activity: “our Board of Directors approved the quarterly cash dividend, which is an 11% increase year-over-year,” and “in the first half of 2026, we bought back a total of $100 million,” with “$498 million available” remaining. He also said the company closed acquisitions “focused on defense, such as Halvik in the U.S. and Providence in Australia.”

Outlook

  • Argus said, “we are increasing our forecasted growth rates for the second half of the year for both our U.S. federal and U.S. commercial client sectors to 8% to 12%,” and noted, “International work, we expect to grow at a 5% to 10% rate,” while “State and local work ... with a growth rate in the high single digits between 5% and 10%.”

  • Guidance was raised: “For the third quarter, net revenue guidance is from $1.05 billion to $1.1 billion” and “adjusted earnings per share guidance is from $0.38 to $0.41.” For FY 2026, “increased net revenue guidance is from $4.25 billion to $4.4 billion” and “increased adjusted earnings per share guidance is from $1.50 to $1.58,” with “this guidance does not include contributions from future acquisitions.”

Financial Results

  • Burdick said “reported revenue was down from last year” due to “the decrease in revenue from USA customers and revenues from onetime disasters this year compared to last year,” while also stating “our operating income increased significantly.”

  • Burdick emphasized cash and working capital outcomes: “cash flows generated from operations for the first half of the year were a historical record at $238 million,” and DSO “58 days,” which he called “an industry-leading standard.”

  • Burdick said leverage improved year-over-year: “net debt on EBITDA was at a leverage of 1.0x,” versus “1.36x” a year ago.

Q&A

  • Timothy Mulrooney, William Blair: asked about sustaining the “8% sequentially” backlog increase and backlog margins; CEO Roger Argus replied, “I believe that Q2 represents an inflection point for Tetra Tech in terms of our backlog,” and said backlog should “support continued margin expansion.”

  • Timothy Mulrooney, William Blair: asked about Canada’s “more than $40 billion” northern/arctic funding; Argus replied, “it is early days,” and said, “I don’t see this as something that’s going to impact us in FY ’26.”

  • Unknown analyst: asked about international demand and capital allocation priority; Argus said demand is “a bit of both” geopolitics and “local demands for water and power,” while CFO Steven Burdick said acquisitions consider “the totality of our balance sheet,” adding, “We’ve never used our equity ... but that doesn’t mean that it’s off the table either.”

  • Sangita Jain, KeyBanc: asked about further DSO opportunity and data center work; Burdick said DSO could move “closer to 50 days,” and Argus said data center work is primarily “feasibility ... associated with siting,” plus some “SCADA systems, commissioning.”

  • Ryan Connors, Northcoast: asked why state and local growth was lowered to “5% to 10%”; Argus cited “caution from our clients related to the proposed federal budgets,” while Burdick said it reflects “pretty close to the same dollars ... but it’s just on a higher base.” Connors also asked about FY ’27 federal budget uncertainty and post-conflict Middle East opportunity; Argus said it is “really too early to comment” on FY ’27, and tied Middle East opportunity to “the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers ... rebuilding damaged facilities.”

  • Maxim Sytchev, National Bank: asked about rising fixed-price mix and M&A philosophy; Burdick said fixed price moved from “about 37%” in 2023 to “about 48%” year-to-date and linked it to “higher margins” and “lower working capital,” while Argus said M&A is “about fit and timing.”

  • Andrew Wittmann, Baird: asked about backlog duration post-USAID and Ukraine; Burdick said there is “more book and burn this year,” confirmed “no onetime disaster revenue,” and said “about $61 million in USA work” was “primarily related to increased activity in Ukraine,” with guidance assuming “about $20 million per quarter” in Q3 and Q4.

  • Tate Sullivan, Maxim: asked if CIG margin can move toward GSG; Argus cited seasonality and said CIG margins should “improve and come back to normal levels,” while Burdick added the backlog shows “increased margins, primarily in CIG.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on funding and visibility, including “proposed federal budgets,” FX/disaster normalization, backlog duration, and USAID/Ukraine.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A on timing-sensitive items, using constraint language such as “it is early days,” “it’s really too early to comment,” and “I don’t see this as something that’s going to impact us in FY ’26.”

  • Compared with last quarter, management discussion shifted from shutdown disruption to backlog acceleration and raised guidance; analyst questioning remained focused on federal funding mechanics, international demand, and capital deployment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Leadership shifted from Dan Batrack’s prepared remarks to Roger Argus leading the call; Argus said it was his “first quarterly call as CEO,” while noting Batrack’s continued role as “Executive Chairman.”

  • Guidance moved higher from Q1’s FY 2026 adjusted EPS range of $1.46-$1.56 and net revenue range of $4.15B-$4.3B to Q2’s $1.50-$1.58 and $4.25B-$4.4B.

  • Backlog commentary shifted from Q1’s “pretty flat backlog” amid a government shutdown to Q2’s “8% sequential growth,” which Argus called an “inflection point.”

  • State and local growth expectations were reduced from Q1’s “10% to 15%” outlook to Q2’s “5% to 10%,” with management attributing the change to client funding caution and base effects.

Risks and Concerns

  • Argus cited uncertainty around FY ’27 federal budgeting: “We’ve seen an initial budget from the White House ... it’s really too early to comment on where the FY ’27 budget might end up.”

  • Burdick and Argus noted revenue headwinds versus last year from “virtually” no disaster-related work and lower USA-related activity, while highlighting mitigation through higher-margin “front-end consulting and design” and increased fixed-price mix.

  • Management flagged timing risk in new international and defense-adjacent opportunities, emphasizing “early days” for Canada’s arctic programs and positioning rather than near-term contribution.

Final Takeaway

Management said it entered the second half of FY 2026 with “strong momentum,” highlighted an “inflection point” in backlog at $4.28 billion, and raised FY 2026 guidance to net revenue of $4.25 billion to $4.4 billion and adjusted EPS of $1.50 to $1.58, while emphasizing record first-half operating cash flow of $238 million, continued margin expansion tied to higher-end consulting and more fixed-price work, and ongoing capital returns alongside defense-focused acquisitions.
